EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of May 26th, parts of northern West Virginia were observing flash flooding as the result thunderstorms that produced heavy downpours the previous evening. Over two and a half inches of rain fell in spots across Taylor and Harrison Counties, which led to high water issues on local roads in the county. Flooding concerns subsided just before sunrise.||Later on that afternoon on the 26th, a bowing line of thunderstorms pressed into the Central Appalachians and caused widespread tree and power line damage. This line of convection had a history of producing strong wind gusts on the upwards of 60 miles per hour or greater as it crossed through the Tennessee Valley, and that remained the case during its journey through West Virginia. Storms first pressed into Mingo and Cabell Counties shortly after 2 PM and then overtook the bulk of the state through the afternoon and evening hours. Storms sprinted northeastward on the evening of the 26th out of the area.||Strong downbursts with storms set forth extensive tree damage throughout the forecast area. An NWS Storm Survey was conducted in Fayette County, finding tree damage indicative of a microburst with peak wind speeds of up to 110 miles per hour. Elsewhere, winds closer to 50-60 miles per hour promoted localized single to multiple downed trees.||A few brief tornadoes spawned on the leading edge of this bowing segment that afternoon. In West Virginia, an EF0 tornado occurred in the town of Winfield in Putnam County, resulting in tree and minor home damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed on Beverly Pike.

The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of May 26th, parts of northern West Virginia were observing flash flooding as the result thunderstorms that produced heavy downpours the previous evening. Over two and a half inches of rain fell in spots across Taylor and Harrison Counties, which led to high water issues on local roads in the county. Flooding concerns subsided just before sunrise.||Later on that afternoon on the 26th, a bowing line of thunderstorms pressed into the Central Appalachians and caused widespread tree and power line damage. This line of convection had a history of producing strong wind gusts on the upwards of 60 miles per hour or greater as it crossed through the Tennessee Valley, and that remained the case during its journey through West Virginia. Storms first pressed into Mingo and Cabell Counties shortly after 2 PM and then overtook the bulk of the state through the afternoon and evening hours. Storms sprinted northeastward on the evening of the 26th out of the area.||Strong downbursts with storms set forth extensive tree damage throughout the forecast area. An NWS Storm Survey was conducted in Fayette County, finding tree damage indicative of a microburst with peak wind speeds of up to 110 miles per hour. Elsewhere, winds closer to 50-60 miles per hour promoted localized single to multiple downed trees.||A few brief tornadoes spawned on the leading edge of this bowing segment that afternoon. In West Virginia, an EF0 tornado occurred in the town of Winfield in Putnam County, resulting in tree and minor home damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed at East Veterans Memorial Highway.

This damage extended along the banks of the Gauley River and up the slopes of the adjacent hillsides. In addition, a roof was blown off of a home along with an attached car port from a different home. The tree damage continued northeast, crossing State Route 39, and ended up a narrow ravine on the north side of the road. It is here where numerous large hardwood trees were uprooted in a general northeast direction.||While there were isolated signs of converging tree damage, likely from steep terrain induced wind patterns, the overwhelming evidence indicated the occurrence of a microburst. This conclusion was further supported by radar interrogation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of May 26th, parts of northern West Virginia were observing flash flooding as the result thunderstorms that produced heavy downpours the previous evening. Over two and a half inches of rain fell in spots across Taylor and Harrison Counties, which led to high water issues on local roads in the county. Flooding concerns subsided just before sunrise.||Later on that afternoon on the 26th, a bowing line of thunderstorms pressed into the Central Appalachians and caused widespread tree and power line damage. This line of convection had a history of producing strong wind gusts on the upwards of 60 miles per hour or greater as it crossed through the Tennessee Valley, and that remained the case during its journey through West Virginia. Storms first pressed into Mingo and Cabell Counties shortly after 2 PM and then overtook the bulk of the state through the afternoon and evening hours. Storms sprinted northeastward on the evening of the 26th out of the area.||Strong downbursts with storms set forth extensive tree damage throughout the forecast area. An NWS Storm Survey was conducted in Fayette County, finding tree damage indicative of a microburst with peak wind speeds of up to 110 miles per hour. Elsewhere, winds closer to 50-60 miles per hour promoted localized single to multiple downed trees.||A few brief tornadoes spawned on the leading edge of this bowing segment that afternoon. In West Virginia, an EF0 tornado occurred in the town of Winfield in Putnam County, resulting in tree and minor home damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees were observed along Grandview Road, including one at the Grandview State Park Amphitheater. One person was injured after falling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracking through the Ohio Valley caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms across the state of West Virginia on April 2nd and 3rd. The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A roughly 500 yard long swath of uprooted trees was observed along Laurel Creek and Saxman Road. Tree damage was divergent, indicative of microburst winds blowing oddly from south to north.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick tornado feeding off of a microburst crossed into Cabell County from Wayne County, producing sporadic straight-line wind damage from the Huntington Airport eastward to Harvey Road. From drone footage, it was apparent that this microburst descended into the north/south oriented valley at 5th Street Road near Woodhaven Drive. Tree and structural damage in this valley showed strong rotation with damage consistent with an EF1 tornado. Several structures were damaged or destroyed and extensive tree damage occurred. The tornado climbed the ridge to the east of 5th Street Road and dissipated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracking through the Ohio Valley caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms across the state of West Virginia on April 2nd and 3rd. The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were snapped on the hillside along Campbells Creek Road.

Over two and a half inches of rain fell in spots across Taylor and Harrison Counties, which led to high water issues on local roads in the county. Flooding concerns subsided just before sunrise.||Later on that afternoon on the 26th, a bowing line of thunderstorms pressed into the Central Appalachians and caused widespread tree and power line damage. This line of convection had a history of producing strong wind gusts on the upwards of 60 miles per hour or greater as it crossed through the Tennessee Valley, and that remained the case during its journey through West Virginia. Storms first pressed into Mingo and Cabell Counties shortly after 2 PM and then overtook the bulk of the state through the afternoon and evening hours. Storms sprinted northeastward on the evening of the 26th out of the area.||Strong downbursts with storms set forth extensive tree damage throughout the forecast area. An NWS Storm Survey was conducted in Fayette County, finding tree damage indicative of a microburst with peak wind speeds of up to 110 miles per hour. Elsewhere, winds closer to 50-60 miles per hour promoted localized single to multiple downed trees.||A few brief tornadoes spawned on the leading edge of this bowing segment that afternoon. In West Virginia, an EF0 tornado occurred in the town of Winfield in Putnam County, resulting in tree and minor home damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down near Dunmore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracking through the Ohio Valley caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms across the state of West Virginia on April 2nd and 3rd. The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down all over Huntington. A tractor trailer was flipped to its side on the 31st Bridge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado with maximum winds of 80 miles per hour was discovered in the Buffalo area. Concentrated tree damage was observed along the side of a ridge along the east side of Railroad Avenue with several large hardwood trees uprooted in a north-northeast orientation, showing a slight converging signature. This signature became more apparent to the northeast along Cross Creek Road where several mixed softwood and hardwood trees were observed either uprooted or snapped. The tornado appeared to stay in the treetops with no property damage observed. The tornado quickly lifted along Cross Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracking through the Ohio Valley caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms across the state of West Virginia on April 2nd and 3rd. The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal roof was removed off of a house with nearby power lines also down near the Dorothy community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of May 26th, parts of northern West Virginia were observing flash flooding as the result thunderstorms that produced heavy downpours the previous evening. Over two and a half inches of rain fell in spots across Taylor and Harrison Counties, which led to high water issues on local roads in the county. Flooding concerns subsided just before sunrise.||Later on that afternoon on the 26th, a bowing line of thunderstorms pressed into the Central Appalachians and caused widespread tree and power line damage. This line of convection had a history of producing strong wind gusts on the upwards of 60 miles per hour or greater as it crossed through the Tennessee Valley, and that remained the case during its journey through West Virginia. Storms first pressed into Mingo and Cabell Counties shortly after 2 PM and then overtook the bulk of the state through the afternoon and evening hours. Storms sprinted northeastward on the evening of the 26th out of the area.||Strong downbursts with storms set forth extensive tree damage throughout the forecast area. An NWS Storm Survey was conducted in Fayette County, finding tree damage indicative of a microburst with peak wind speeds of up to 110 miles per hour. Elsewhere, winds closer to 50-60 miles per hour promoted localized single to multiple downed trees.||A few brief tornadoes spawned on the leading edge of this bowing segment that afternoon. In West Virginia, an EF0 tornado occurred in the town of Winfield in Putnam County, resulting in tree and minor home damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree blew down and blocked Millstone Road outside the Apple Grove township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front swinging through West Virginia set forth afternoon showers and thunderstorms on May 22nd. This was especially the case for areas along the I-64 corridor, where storm damage was observed from strong wind gusts and hail. Winds associated with convection were powerful enough to blow off portions of roofs on homes in the Lesage area. As storms tracked further into the central and eastern parts of the state, the threat level tapered down and no additional damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in the Pea Ridge area, along with power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 25th, a swath of downed trees occurred across north-central West Virginia as passing disturbances provided support to a nearby stalled frontal boundary. Scattered showers and storms arrived into the state from the west and grew to become strong to severe. Most notable damage that occurred from the resulting damaging winds were downed trees in Harrison, Taylor, and Barbour Counties. ||Heavy rain in these storms also caused one road in Barbour County to flood. Preceding rounds of rain from days prior had already made soils more susceptible to flash flooding. High water issues eventually came to an end after precipitation departed to the east the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed tree was observed at the intersection of Route 119 and Upper Pleasant Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Active weather took place each afternoon in West Virginia from April 15th to the 17th in the midst of a slow moving upper level disturbance. Isolated to scattered thunderstorms resulted in instances of downed trees and power lines from damaging wind gusts and hail stones ranging from nickel size to golf ball size. Storms were primarily driven by daytime heating, weakening in intensity each evening after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous areas of downed trees were observed in South Charleston, including at Ridge Drive, Kirby Street, and Richmond Road. The roof of a deck also collapsed at a home in South Charleston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracking through the Ohio Valley caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms across the state of West Virginia on April 2nd and 3rd. The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down from Frametown to Gassaway and Sutton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of April 11th due to the combination of unseasonably warm temperatures and a passing disturbance. Storms crossed in from the Ohio River Valley during the early afternoon and continued an eastward progression into the evening. A swath of tree damage and power outages were observed from Mingo County to the eastern half of the West Virginia from damaging wind gusts. Storms pressed eastward late on the evening of April 11th.||In addition, numerous areas of flooding transpired that afternoon and evening in response to heavy downpours and training storms throughout the state. The Charleston airport set records for the wettest April as a result of observing 3.32 inches of rainfall on April 11th, breaking the old record of 1.83 inches set back in 1965.||High water issues along state roads were the main impacts from the flooding, some of which needed water rescues for cars that drove into these trouble spots. Excess water eventually drained into the Ohio River on the 12th and 13th of April, returning to its banks a few days later. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 14th, when a motorist drove past high water signs and their vehicle became submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Middle Island Creek near Middlebourne exceeded bankfull on the night of April 11th. The creek continued to rise to a crest of around 24.1 feet on the afternoon of the 12th, then fell returned to its banks again that night. Major flooding occurred along routes 18 and 24 on either side of Middlebourne. 4H camp buildings, county fairgrounds, and the agricultural field near the gauge flooded while the creek was out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather was observed across the state on both of May 9th and 11th as the result of a crossing cold front and subsequent upper level disturbances. ||Starting on the afternoon of the 9th, strong to severe thunderstorms first swept through northwest West Virginia, knocking down trees due to their resulting damaging wind gusts. A brief EF1 tornado touched down near the Parkersburg area that evening, causing multiple areas of tree damage within its path, along with an RV that was windswept onto its side.||Additional storms flared up two days later on the 11th, yielding additional tree damage across northern and parts of southeastern West Virginia. A few instances of quarter size hail was also observed within storms that afternoon.||Convection both evenings quickly diminished with the loss of daytime heating after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF1 tornado occurred east of Parkersburg on the evening of May 9th. Initial concentrated tree damage north of Rising Sun Ridge Road was found during the storm survey. There were hardwood trees observed uprooted with additional large branches down in a converging pattern.||The damage continued southeast to a small section of Jericho Road. Here, a narrow swath of large mixed hardwood and softwood trees were observed either uprooted or snapped close to their bases. In addition, a mobile home and barn were flipped over. There was one minor injury, as a person was inside the mobile home at the time it flipped over. There was a clear converging damage pattern noted here. Tree damage continued southeast to the end of Whitlatch Lane where large mixed hardwood and softwood trees were uprooted or snapped in a converging pattern. The highest rated damage occurred at these locations and served as the basis for the EF1 rating.||The tornado continued southeast to Ralston Hill Road and weakened, as only a few trees were uprooted or snapped with primarily large branches down. The tornado lifted along the southern end of State Route 31, just north of State Highway 50. A few trees were uprooted with large branches down along with an RV that was rolled over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather was observed across the state on both of May 9th and 11th as the result of a crossing cold front and subsequent upper level disturbances. ||Starting on the afternoon of the 9th, strong to severe thunderstorms first swept through northwest West Virginia, knocking down trees due to their resulting damaging wind gusts. A brief EF1 tornado touched down near the Parkersburg area that evening, causing multiple areas of tree damage within its path, along with an RV that was windswept onto its side.||Additional storms flared up two days later on the 11th, yielding additional tree damage across northern and parts of southeastern West Virginia. A few instances of quarter size hail was also observed within storms that afternoon.||Convection both evenings quickly diminished with the loss of daytime heating after sunset.

A strong area of low pressure tracking through the Ohio Valley caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms across the state of West Virginia on April 2nd and 3rd. The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tygart Valley River at Philippi rose above its 19 foot flood stage on the evening of April 3rd. The river continued to rise to a crest of around 19.7 feet that night and then returned below flood stage the next morning. This resulted in minor flooding at the intersection of US Route 119 and Truck Route 250, as well as at Sycamore Street and Beech Avenue in Philippi.

The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Middle Fork Tygart Valley River at Ellamore rose above its 13 foot bankfull level on the afternoon of April 3rd. The river continued to rise to a crest of around 14.8 feet and returned to its banks the same evening. Portions of County Route 28 near the Randolph/Upshur County line observed high water issues while the river was out of its banks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system spread scattered showers and storms across West Virginia on the morning of May 14th, with more substantial rainfall amounts accrued from a wave of showers and storms later that evening and night. In Wayne County, two and a half to three inches of rain fell across the southern portion of the county and led to flooding along Cabwaylingo Park Road. Flooding ceased as precipitation lifted north before midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was observed on Cabwaylingo Park Road in the Doane township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance and stationary front at the surface provided a focus for rounds of showers and storms on May 24th. Locally heavy rain fell across portions of West Virginia, which had already been saturated by rounds of rain the previous day. Pockets of rain totaling between one and a half to three inches accrued across portions of Kanawha and Logan Counties and resulted in flash flooding on a few roadways early in the morning. Flooding came to an end as rain exited to the east by mid morning. A few hours later, convection began to develop in the vicinity of a front across northern West Virginia and led to additional flooding in Taylor County during the evening of the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water reported in the 7100 block of Sissonville Drive in the Happy Hollow area and at the Pocatalico entrance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On May 25th, a swath of downed trees occurred across north-central West Virginia as passing disturbances provided support to a nearby stalled frontal boundary. Scattered showers and storms arrived into the state from the west and grew to become strong to severe. Most notable damage that occurred from the resulting damaging winds were downed trees in Harrison, Taylor, and Barbour Counties. ||Heavy rain in these storms also caused one road in Barbour County to flood. Preceding rounds of rain from days prior had already made soils more susceptible to flash flooding. High water issues eventually came to an end after precipitation departed to the east the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed on Cherokee Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of isolated thunderstorms produced localized wind damage and large hail, mainly over southern and western Maryland, and the eastern West Virginia panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to half dollar size was observed on Jimtown Road near Berkeley Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of April 11th due to the combination of unseasonably warm temperatures and a passing disturbance. Storms crossed in from the Ohio River Valley during the early afternoon and continued an eastward progression into the evening. A swath of tree damage and power outages were observed from Mingo County to the eastern half of the West Virginia from damaging wind gusts. Storms pressed eastward late on the evening of April 11th.||In addition, numerous areas of flooding transpired that afternoon and evening in response to heavy downpours and training storms throughout the state. The Charleston airport set records for the wettest April as a result of observing 3.32 inches of rainfall on April 11th, breaking the old record of 1.83 inches set back in 1965.||High water issues along state roads were the main impacts from the flooding, some of which needed water rescues for cars that drove into these trouble spots. Excess water eventually drained into the Ohio River on the 12th and 13th of April, returning to its banks a few days later. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 14th, when a motorist drove past high water signs and their vehicle became submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on Market Road near Pinewood Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions over the eastern panhandle of West Virginia let to gradual deterioration in drought conditions. Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought was expanded into Tucker county, WV at the start of July and deteriorated further to Extreme drought (D3) by the middle of the month. By July 16th, severe drought was expanded to include the northern panhandle of WV including portions of Wetzel, Marshall, and Ohio counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: D2 conditions started at the beginning of July, expanding to D3 conditions by July 16th. Extreme drought continued until August 13, where some beneficial rain supported a return to D2 conditions. D3 conditions returned on September 17th and continue at this time.

Storms pressed eastward late on the evening of April 11th.||In addition, numerous areas of flooding transpired that afternoon and evening in response to heavy downpours and training storms throughout the state. The Charleston airport set records for the wettest April as a result of observing 3.32 inches of rainfall on April 11th, breaking the old record of 1.83 inches set back in 1965.||High water issues along state roads were the main impacts from the flooding, some of which needed water rescues for cars that drove into these trouble spots. Excess water eventually drained into the Ohio River on the 12th and 13th of April, returning to its banks a few days later. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 14th, when a motorist drove past high water signs and their vehicle became submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Tygart Creek at Mineral Wells climbed above its 11 foot bankfull level on the afternoon of April 12th, then continued to rise to a crest of around 15.3 feet the following evening. Backwater from of the Ohio River sustained flooding along Little Tygart Creek until the morning of the 15th. This level of flooding impacted most of Speedway Road and sections of Woodridge Drive and Dickens Drive. Flooding also reached a home on Mayo Drive above the intersection with Irene Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracking through the Ohio Valley caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms across the state of West Virginia on April 2nd and 3rd. The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with maximum winds of 105 miles per hour endured a track of about 4 miles, extending from just south of Route 2 near the Guyandotte River to just west of the Barboursville Mall. This was a non-continuous track with several notable damage locations.||A narrow concentrated area of hardwood tree damage was noted on a hilltop along Viewpoint Lane. Several of the trees were either snapped or uprooted in a converging pattern and continued southeast along Estate Drive.||Significant mixed hardwood and softwood tree damage was observed along Guyan River Road with most of the intense damage occurring along the banks of the Guyandotte River. Numerous large hardwood and softwood trees were either snapped or uprooted. Damage to a mobile home occurred due to a large fallen tree. In addition, damage to the roof of an apartment complex on the other side of the river on Beech Drive occurred from a fallen tree. This section of the track is where the maximum winds are believed to have occurred.||The tornado path continued eastward with a distinct damage swath of hardwood trees noted along Merritts Creek Road. Large hardwood trees were either snapped or uprooted in a converging pattern. A small mobile office unit was blown in the opposite direction of the orientation of the tree damage. From here, the tornado reached its maximum width of 200 yards as it entered into a valley. Tree damage continued east up the ridgetop along Big Ben Bowen Highway before ending along Wildcat Road. The tornado is believed to have lifted before reaching the Barboursville Mall, which is supported by archived radar images. ||This tornado, as with many of the others on this day, stayed mainly in the treetops with little in the way of property damage observed.||Outside of the tornado path, rear flank downdraft wind damage occurred east and southeast of the tornado, where a concrete block wall attached to the exterior of the mall was blown apart to the northeast. In addition, part of the roof of a business just east of the mall was lifted and blown to the northeast as well. Finally, scattered tree damage was observed on both sides of Interstate 64 east of the mall, with a general orientation to the northeast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of April 11th due to the combination of unseasonably warm temperatures and a passing disturbance. Storms crossed in from the Ohio River Valley during the early afternoon and continued an eastward progression into the evening. A swath of tree damage and power outages were observed from Mingo County to the eastern half of the West Virginia from damaging wind gusts. Storms pressed eastward late on the evening of April 11th.||In addition, numerous areas of flooding transpired that afternoon and evening in response to heavy downpours and training storms throughout the state. The Charleston airport set records for the wettest April as a result of observing 3.32 inches of rainfall on April 11th, breaking the old record of 1.83 inches set back in 1965.||High water issues along state roads were the main impacts from the flooding, some of which needed water rescues for cars that drove into these trouble spots. Excess water eventually drained into the Ohio River on the 12th and 13th of April, returning to its banks a few days later. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 14th, when a motorist drove past high water signs and their vehicle became submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed at several locations in Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass combined with an approaching cold front helped to spark an isolated thunderstorm that became severe across Greenbrier County. Thunderstorm winds blew down a notable number of trees and also blew a roof off a garage and pushed it away by 500 feet. Hail up to the size of quarters also fell from this storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees and snapped off several more trees near Red Horse Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions over the eastern panhandle of West Virginia let to gradual deterioration in drought conditions. Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought was expanded into Tucker county, WV at the start of July and deteriorated further to Extreme drought (D3) by the middle of the month. By July 16th, severe drought was expanded to include the northern panhandle of WV including portions of Wetzel, Marshall, and Ohio counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions were expanded into the northern panhandle on the 16th. Beneficial rains of 2-4 inches resulted in improvement on July 30th back to Moderate Drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex swept through West Virginia on the afternoon and evening of July 5th in response to an upper level disturbance. Storms advanced eastward through the Ohio Valley and reached the western flank of the state that evening, bringing destructive winds as convection presented itself in a bowing segment. This resulted in areas of downed trees and power lines across north-central West Virginia before activity departed into the Mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down in the Kenna area, one falling at the intersection of Zion Ridge Road and Wilbern Road, and the other along Statts Mills Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoons of July 14th and 16th, eastward progressing thunderstorm complexes ventured into West Virginia and brought strong to severe storms through the state. Downed trees and power lines were most common on both days as damaging wind gusts accompanied the convection. The risk for severe weather remained brief both days as activity quickly pivoted east into Virginia and Maryland by the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A fallen tree blocked the intersection of Woodland Road and Lieving Road.

A swath of tree damage and power outages were observed from Mingo County to the eastern half of the West Virginia from damaging wind gusts. Storms pressed eastward late on the evening of April 11th.||In addition, numerous areas of flooding transpired that afternoon and evening in response to heavy downpours and training storms throughout the state. The Charleston airport set records for the wettest April as a result of observing 3.32 inches of rainfall on April 11th, breaking the old record of 1.83 inches set back in 1965.||High water issues along state roads were the main impacts from the flooding, some of which needed water rescues for cars that drove into these trouble spots. Excess water eventually drained into the Ohio River on the 12th and 13th of April, returning to its banks a few days later. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 14th, when a motorist drove past high water signs and their vehicle became submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding from the Pocatalico River impacted Cicerone Road in Walton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoons of July 14th and 16th, eastward progressing thunderstorm complexes ventured into West Virginia and brought strong to severe storms through the state. Downed trees and power lines were most common on both days as damaging wind gusts accompanied the convection. The risk for severe weather remained brief both days as activity quickly pivoted east into Virginia and Maryland by the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were broken off of a tree outside the Burlington township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracking through the Ohio Valley caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms across the state of West Virginia on April 2nd and 3rd. The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ohio River at Parkersburg rose above flood stage on the afternoon of April 3rd, then continued to rise to a crest of around 40.1 feet the morning of the 5th. The river then fell back below flood stage the afternoon of April 6th. This extensive amount of time above flood stage resulted in flooding of several homes in the Happy Valley and Point Drive areas near Parkersburg as well as sections of Vienna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and humid conditions overspread much of the region on July 15 and July 16, resulting in the hottest temperatures of the Summer in 2024. Heat index values reached and exceeded 100 to 110, with daily record high temperatures tied and set.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at Eastern West Virginia Airport in Martinsburg (KMRB) measured a peak heat index value of 102, with a high temperature of 100F. Several mesonets in the county measured peak heat indices around 105.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the midst of a stationary frontal boundary promoting showers and storms across West Virginia on the morning of July 22nd, an isolated instance of flash flooding occurred in the Beckley area of West Virginia. Heavy rainfall accompanying convective activity early that morning yielded minor creek flooding in Raleigh County, which spilled onto Robert C Byrd Drive where it intersects with Ewart Avenue. High water issues dragged on into the morning rush hour before gradually tapering down in response to the creek returning to its banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was observed at the intersection of Robert C Byrd Drive and Ewart Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions over the eastern panhandle of West Virginia let to gradual deterioration in drought conditions. Moderate (D1) to Severe (D2) drought was expanded into Tucker county, WV at the start of July and deteriorated further to Extreme drought (D3) by the middle of the month. By July 16th, severe drought was expanded to include the northern panhandle of WV including portions of Wetzel, Marshall, and Ohio counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought was expanded to western Tucker county on July 9th and deteriorated further to Extreme Drought on July 16th. Some improvement occurred at the beginning of August due to beneficial rainfall and D2 conditions returned August 13th until August 27th when conditions were deteriorated back to D3.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe Drought conditions were expanded into the northern panhandle on the 16th. Beneficial rains of 2-4 inches resulted in improvement on July 30th back to Moderate Drought conditions.

The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down west of Mallard Road then traveled eastward along the roadway, causing considerable and significant tree damage with hundreds of hardwood trees uprooted and snapped. Several homes were damaged by trees or had wind damaging along Mallard Road, including one home that had significant roof damage.||The tornado continued eastward crossing Lookout Road and US 60. The tornado reached peak width of 325 yards here and peak wind speeds were likely reached just west of Lookout Road where tree damage indicated wind speeds estimated at 130 miles per hour. This tornado continued to damage or destroy several homes and businesses along US 60. One home was shifted several feet off of its foundation and the metal roof was tossed approximately 350 yards to the tree line across US 60.||The tornado continued its journey east of US 60 with a path of tree damage noted for another mile before aerial photography from the West Virginia Civil Air Patrol indicated that it lifted. Additional damage was subsequently found approximately 3 miles to the east along Bracken Creek Road where a small building was destroyed and a path of tree damage was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms prompted a widespread flash flood problems across the Ohio River Valley on April 2nd to April 3rd. The synoptic pattern across the CONUS was a slow moving and deep elongated trough over the Great Plains phasing with a ejecting trough over the Midwest that created an area of converging moisture over eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania. Overall, over a span of 4 days, four to five an half inches of rain fell across the region. Not only was small tributaries impact, but the main river reached moderate flood stage. ||Along with flooding, there was a brief period of severe storms that tracked along eastern Ohio on the evening of April 2nd. A number of tornadoes touched down and were surveyed. Special soundings released by PBZ and ILN depict effective instability over eastern Ohio (1200J/kg to 1700J/kg), effective low-level shear (+50kts), and low-level helicity (300m2/s2) for rotating updrafts just before storms tracked through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Shortline Highway was closed due to flooding. Wetzel County declared $933,000 in damage from storms between April 2-6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed in western Ohio on April 17th along a cold front before tracking east into western Pennsylvania. Behind the cold front, a 50kt low-level jet was measured on the KPBZ radar at 3,000ft and surface based instability ranged between 500J/kg to 1,000J/kg. As a result, widespread wind damage was noted with this passing system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on power lines on Allison Road. Time estimated from radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough and an associated frontal zone crossed through the|Mid-South and took aim on the OH Valley. Strong forcing associated the energy aloft coupled with a gradually destabilizing boundary layer out ahead it set the stage for areas of locally heavy showers and thunderstorms for several hours and prompted dangerous flash flooding. A flash flood emergency was issued for portions of Allegheny County when 4 to 5 inches of rain fell on the community of Oakdale, PA in less than 6 hours.  Elsewhere, flash flooding occurred along small tributaries with widespread rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report of evacuating 4 people from a home due to high water and potential landslide. Wetzel County declared $1,423,900 in damage from storms between April 11-12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early afternoon convection developed along a slow moving frontal boundary in northern West Virginia. An isolated storm had severe hail observed due to high effective wind shear (+40kts) and instability ranging between 750J/kg to 1500J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar sized hail reported near Jo Harry Drive and Country Club Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large scale low pressure system over the entire East Coast created winter weather impacts over the ridges of West Virginia April 4th to April 7th. Due to the prolong period of northwest flow, orographic lift with sufficient over the Appalachians. Over 6 inches snow fell in the higher terrain of Tucker County in a 24 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches of snow was measured in the ridges of eastern Tucker county the morning of April 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough and an associated frontal zone crossed through the|Mid-South and took aim on the OH Valley. Strong forcing associated the energy aloft coupled with a gradually destabilizing boundary layer out ahead it set the stage for areas of locally heavy showers and thunderstorms for several hours and prompted dangerous flash flooding. A flash flood emergency was issued for portions of Allegheny County when 4 to 5 inches of rain fell on the community of Oakdale, PA in less than 6 hours.  Elsewhere, flash flooding occurred along small tributaries with widespread rainfall amounts of 2 to 3 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy water running across Roadway 1 up to Proctor Creek from Route 2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training showers created isolated flooding in portions of the region early Monday morning (April 1st). Convection originated in the Midwest along a stationary boundary. With zonal flow over Illinois, Indiana, and Ohio, due to an elongated trough over the West Coast, convection trained from west to east. CoCoRaHS observers measured 1.50 to 2 inches of rain along I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along Little Grave Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front, making eastward progress through the Ohio Valley, helped to promote showers and thunderstorms through West Virginia on June 26th. Despite excessive cloud coverage, peaks of sunshine aided in daytime heating and the resulting development of strong to severe thunderstorms across the state. Outside of an isolated instance of quarter size hail in Clay County, the strongest storms that day caused downed trees and power lines due to damaging wind gusts. Convection jetted off to the northeast that evening with the encroachment of the cold front, bringing severe weather to an end.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large branches from a tree fell down into the yard of a home in Cross Lanes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the midst of abnormally hot conditions around the region, strong to severe thunderstorms thrived across West Virginia on June 29th and 30th. On both days, storms initially developed during peak heating hours and progressed through the state before diminishing with the loss of daytime heating. Storm damage was mainly observed in the form of downed trees each day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One downed tree was observed on Pennsboro Industrial Park Road, which blocked both lanes of traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heat wave festering over the Central Appalachians kept the door open for daily chances of shower and thunderstorm development during peak heating hours on the afternoons of June 17th and 18th. This led to instances of downed trees both days across parts of West Virginia. A strong high pressure system caused there to be little to no winds throughout the atmospheric profile, which promoted slow storm movement and ultimate decaying of convection shortly after initializing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the town of Red Jacket, a tree blew down onto Mate Creek Road. Another tree took down power lines along Mitchell Branch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved across southeast West Virginia during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the day. Damaging winds from the storms downed power lines and a large number of trees, blew a roof off a building, and caused superficial damage to a couple other buildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew numerous trees down around Rupert. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front crossed the area which helped sparked scattered showers and storms. One of these storms increased to severe levels and produced one-inch diameter hail near Matoaka.

Showers and storms developed in advance of an approaching cold front. Some of these storms strengthened enough to produce hail ranging from penny to quarter size, and winds strong enough to down power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed power lines within the 600 block of Gardner Road. Damage values are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of upslope snow took place due to a cutoff low that developed over New England. There was a large spread in accumulation amounts relative to elevation. For elevations above 3,500 feet, total snow accumulation around 6 to 12 inches occurred. For elevations between 2,500 and 3,500 feet, accumulations around 1 to 3 inches were observed with lesser amounts below 2,500 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracking through the Ohio Valley caused multiple rounds of thunderstorms across the state of West Virginia on April 2nd and 3rd. The first round that occurred on the 2nd took the form of an intense squall line that raced across the southern and central portions of the state during the late morning and midday hours. This caused extensive wind damage, with a 92 mile per hour wind gust measured at the Huntington Tri-State Airport, along with at least nine embedded tornadoes in a corridor from Huntington to east of Charleston. In addition to damage associated with the tornadoes, over 100 locations reported non-tornadic wind damage.||Additional thunderstorms later in the afternoon produced another tornado in the Hico community of Fayette County. The ten confirmed tornadoes during this event is a record for the most tornadoes observed during any event in the state of West Virginia.||To top it all off, the multiple rounds of thunderstorms also resulted in excessive rainfall amounts for parts of the state. This resulted in several instances of flash flooding of creeks and streams with reported road closures due to high water, primarily during the evening of April 2nd. The transition to generalized flooding occurred during the early morning of April 3rd due to the response from larger creeks. Considerable runoff from this rainfall caused river flooding along the Tygart Valley River and its tributaries, and very heavy rainfall over Pennsylvania from these same storms led to moderate to major flooding on the Ohio River between April 4th to the 6th. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 4th when a motorist entered flood waters and their vehicle overturned and then submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a house on Meadowbrook Circle in Teays Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed on the afternoon of April 11th due to the combination of unseasonably warm temperatures and a passing disturbance. Storms crossed in from the Ohio River Valley during the early afternoon and continued an eastward progression into the evening. A swath of tree damage and power outages were observed from Mingo County to the eastern half of the West Virginia from damaging wind gusts. Storms pressed eastward late on the evening of April 11th.||In addition, numerous areas of flooding transpired that afternoon and evening in response to heavy downpours and training storms throughout the state. The Charleston airport set records for the wettest April as a result of observing 3.32 inches of rainfall on April 11th, breaking the old record of 1.83 inches set back in 1965.||High water issues along state roads were the main impacts from the flooding, some of which needed water rescues for cars that drove into these trouble spots. Excess water eventually drained into the Ohio River on the 12th and 13th of April, returning to its banks a few days later. One fatality occurred in Wood County on the 14th, when a motorist drove past high water signs and their vehicle became submerged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred outside several structures in the town of Pennsboro, and along East Washington Avenue and Lamberton Road in the town of Ellenboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from the remnants of Tropical Storm Helene led to an extended period of river flooding that lingered into the beginning of October.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A river gauge on the Shenandoah River at Millville reached minor flood stage (10 feet), with the gauge cresting at 12.04 feet. At 10 feet, parts of Bloomery Road and John Rissler Road are flooded near Bloomery, West Virginia. Once these roads flood, access to homes is impaired but the homes themselves are not in any danger of flooding. Moulton Park and the Millville boat launch are also flooded. At 11 feet, a small portion of Wilt Road (County Road 9/8) begins to flood. Water also begins to flow over Millville Dam. At 12 feet, water reaches the river access at Wagon Trail Place near Harpers Ferry, and completely covers the parking lot of the lower boat ramp at Millville Dam. Portions of the Cool Spring Battlefield begin to flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper low centered over northern Lake Michigan slowly shifted east during the afternoon, and the approach of a pre-frontal trough combined with diurnal destabilization resulted in convective development along and ahead of a cold frontal boundary.  MLCAPE values near 1000-2000 J/kg and mid-level lapse rates of 6-6.5 C/km resulted in a damaging wind threat with convection.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tress were reported down in Weirton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of October 6th, a cold front promoted isolated showers and thunderstorms across West Virginia. One particular storm that evening crossed through both Ritchie and Doddridge Counties and resulted in downed trees due to the strong winds it caused. With the loss of daytime heating, radar activity quickly weakened in the state and departed to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Dogwood Lane outside the town of West Union.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: October began with at least abnormally dry (D0) conditions touching every county in the state. The worst drought conditions (D3 to D4) covered portions of northern, western, and central West Virginia, while conditions gradually improved to the south and east. Only one county, McDowell, had no drought in half of the county, while D0 covered the other half. ||Several systems brought cold fronts through the area during the month. However, the majority of the area only received a few hundredths of rain at a time, while pockets of an inch or more were infrequently observed. Multiple days of dry weather in between systems also prevented whatever precipitation that did fall from putting a significant dent in the drought. Climatologically speaking, October was an abnormally dry month with all six climate sites averaging precipitation amounts between one to three inches below normal. The climate sites at Beckley and Huntington both experienced their second driest October on record, while Charleston experienced the third driest October on record.||By the end of the month, the ���no drought��� area in McDowell County had descended into the abnormally dry category, while the severe to exceptional drought spanning the bulk of the state remained relatively unchanged in coverage.||Impacts reported during the height of the drought included below normal stream flows, low water in wells and reservoirs, a shortage of hay, water hauling, and an increase in fire danger.

Dryness expanded across much of southeast West Virginia during the second half of August given spotty rainfall totals ranging from 0.5 to 1.5 inches falling from August 13th through the end of the month. Much of this was the result of brief but heavy rain during passing thunderstorm activity that was often localized. This prevented soil moisture and streamflows from benefiting for more than a few days after the rain event, with dryness then quickly returning. By the end of the month, soil moistures ranged from the 10th to the 30th percentiles, while streamflows were falling increasingly below normal. Pastureland grasses were reported to be insufficient to keep cattle fed, causing farmers to supplement hay much earlier than they normally would. Some farmers were also reportedly making plans to sell livestock as they did not have the resources to keep them through the winter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dryness expanded across much of Summers County during the second half of August given spotty rainfall totals ranging from 0.5 to 1.25 inches falling from August 13th through the end of the month.  Much of this was the result of brief but heavy rain during passing thunderstorm activity that was often localized.  This prevented soil moisture and streamflows from benefiting for more than a few days after the rain event, with dryness then quickly returning.  By the end of the month, soil moisture across Summers County ranged from the 10th to the 20th percentiles, while streamflows had fallen below the 25th percentile.  Pastureland grasses were reported to be insufficient to keep cattle fed, causing farmers to supplement hay much earlier than they normally would.  Some farmers were also reportedly making plans to sell livestock as they did not have the resources to keep them through the winter.

On the evening of October 6th, a cold front promoted isolated showers and thunderstorms across West Virginia. One particular storm that evening crossed through both Ritchie and Doddridge Counties and resulted in downed trees due to the strong winds it caused. With the loss of daytime heating, radar activity quickly weakened in the state and departed to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down across Laurel Creek Road near the town of North Hills.

With the loss of daytime heating, radar activity quickly weakened in the state and departed to the east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Pennsboro, a tree was knocked down and blocked Cunningham Run Road.

A shortwave trough over the Great Lakes interacting with a quasi-stationary boundary over eastern Ohio and western PA spawned severe weather from mid afternoon through late evening. MLCAPE near 2000 J/kg along with a relatively strong, elongated shear profiles led to an environment for strong to severe winds and possible tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State officials reported numerous trees down.

Strong southwesterly wind ahead of an approaching front eventually advected sufficient moisture and warm air in the lower levels to overcome a capping inversion ahead of a cold front that would eventually be the  main driver of forcing to take advantage of a supportive environment. Damaging wind was the primary threat, although the shear and lapse rates aloft pointed towards a limited potential for large hail as well. Tornadoes looked possible, however, LCLs were relatively high, and any issued TORs did not verify.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Officials reported trees down along Blue Horizon Drive.

Damaging wind was the primary threat, although the shear and lapse rates aloft pointed towards a limited potential for large hail as well. Tornadoes looked possible, however, LCLs were relatively high, and any issued TORs did not verify.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Received reports of a few trees down near Morgantown.

The worst drought conditions (D3 to D4) covered portions of northern, western, and central West Virginia, while conditions gradually improved to the south and east. Only one county, McDowell, had no drought in half of the county, while D0 covered the other half. ||Several systems brought cold fronts through the area during the month. However, the majority of the area only received a few hundredths of rain at a time, while pockets of an inch or more were infrequently observed. Multiple days of dry weather in between systems also prevented whatever precipitation that did fall from putting a significant dent in the drought. Climatologically speaking, October was an abnormally dry month with all six climate sites averaging precipitation amounts between one to three inches below normal. The climate sites at Beckley and Huntington both experienced their second driest October on record, while Charleston experienced the third driest October on record.||By the end of the month, the ���no drought��� area in McDowell County had descended into the abnormally dry category, while the severe to exceptional drought spanning the bulk of the state remained relatively unchanged in coverage.||Impacts reported during the height of the drought included below normal stream flows, low water in wells and reservoirs, a shortage of hay, water hauling, and an increase in fire danger.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak front analyzed north over NY into MI dropped south as shortwave crossed a flattening ridge aloft. Where cloud cover was not as prevalent, convective temperatures were reached, and with relatively high DCAPE values and effective shear between 20-30kts, there was potential for damaging wind with stronger storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along Big Run Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dryness expanded across much of southeast West Virginia during the second half of August given spotty rainfall totals ranging from 0.5 to 1.5 inches falling from August 13th through the end of the month. Much of this was the result of brief but heavy rain during passing thunderstorm activity that was often localized. This prevented soil moisture and streamflows from benefiting for more than a few days after the rain event, with dryness then quickly returning. By the end of the month, soil moistures ranged from the 10th to the 30th percentiles, while streamflows were falling increasingly below normal. Pastureland grasses were reported to be insufficient to keep cattle fed, causing farmers to supplement hay much earlier than they normally would. Some farmers were also reportedly making plans to sell livestock as they did not have the resources to keep them through the winter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dryness expanded across much of Greenbrier County during the second half of August given spotty rainfall totals ranging from 0.5 to 1.5 inches falling from August 13th through the end of the month.  Much of this was the result of brief but heavy rain during passing thunderstorm activity that was often localized.  This prevented soil moisture and streamflows from benefiting for more than a few days after the rain event, with dryness then quickly returning.  By the end of the month, soil moisture across eastern Greenbrier County ranged from the 10th to the 30th percentiles, while streamflows were falling increasingly below normal.  Pastureland grasses were reported to be insufficient to keep cattle fed, causing farmers to supplement hay much earlier than they normally would.  Some farmers were also reportedly making plans to sell livestock as they did not have the resources to keep them through the winter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a quiet end to the month of July, exceptionally dry weather prevailed through the majority of August and attributed to a historical drought across West Virginia. The month began with severe drought (D2) conditions laid across the north-central parts of the state and gradually grew worse through the course of the month. Under strong high pressure and daily afternoon temperatures stretching into the triple digit marks, soil conditions continued to dry out. By the middle of August, many locations in the Kanawha Valley and up the I-77/I-79 corridors were expanded to an extreme drought (D3) state. For the first time in history since the establishment of the Drought Monitor, a D4 exceptional drought category was introduced in parts of Mason, Jackson, Wood, Wirt, Roane, and Putnam Counties.||Declining levels in irritation wells/ponds in August led to hauling of water for livestock across the lowlands of West Virginia. Other impacts from this drought also included a crop yield loss reported across much of western and central West Virginia as well as nearby hay shortages. Given the drying vegetation and above normal temperatures, brush fire activity also increased by the end of the second half of the month. Several reservoir lake levels were also below their summer pools in the state.||Exceptionally dry conditions stretched into September.

Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms progressed through West Virginia on August 31st as a cold front swept through the Ohio Valley. While the overall setup for severe weather was low that day, a few storms grew strong enough to produce downbursts that resulted in single tree damage across West Virginia. In addition, a few spots observed stronger intensity damaging wind gusts, which caused multiple downed trees. As the cold front inched closer to Central Appalachia, the thunderstorm threat weakened in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down onto Ultra Lane in Ashton.

After a quiet end to the month of July, exceptionally dry weather prevailed through the majority of August and attributed to a historical drought across West Virginia. The month began with severe drought (D2) conditions laid across the north-central parts of the state and gradually grew worse through the course of the month. Under strong high pressure and daily afternoon temperatures stretching into the triple digit marks, soil conditions continued to dry out. By the middle of August, many locations in the Kanawha Valley and up the I-77/I-79 corridors were expanded to an extreme drought (D3) state. For the first time in history since the establishment of the Drought Monitor, a D4 exceptional drought category was introduced in parts of Mason, Jackson, Wood, Wirt, Roane, and Putnam Counties.||Declining levels in irritation wells/ponds in August led to hauling of water for livestock across the lowlands of West Virginia. Other impacts from this drought also included a crop yield loss reported across much of western and central West Virginia as well as nearby hay shortages. Given the drying vegetation and above normal temperatures, brush fire activity also increased by the end of the second half of the month. Several reservoir lake levels were also below their summer pools in the state.||Exceptionally dry conditions stretched into September.

Several rounds of showers and thunderstorms progressed through West Virginia on August 31st as a cold front swept through the Ohio Valley. While the overall setup for severe weather was low that day, a few storms grew strong enough to produce downbursts that resulted in single tree damage across West Virginia. In addition, a few spots observed stronger intensity damaging wind gusts, which caused multiple downed trees. As the cold front inched closer to Central Appalachia, the thunderstorm threat weakened in West Virginia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Hurricane Creek Road in Fraziers Bottom.

The last noted damage was along the 200 block of Mound Builder Loop where several trees were uprooted and one snapped.
